vocabulary.subtitle: God Almighty — More Than Enough
vocabulary.overview: El Shaddai is the name God used when He appeared to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. It speaks of God as the All-Sufficient One — not merely powerful but abundantly, overflowingly enough. When life is impossible, when the body is too old, when the situation is beyond human ability — El Shaddai steps in. This is the name of miraculous provision, the name under which Sarah's barren womb was opened and Isaac was born.

content.meaning: God Almighty — the All-Sufficient One. El (God of power) + Shaddai (possibly from shad meaning breast — nourisher, or from shadad meaning to overpower). The name speaks of God as the One who is more than enough for every need, who nourishes and sustains His people through impossible circumstances.

practice.question: To whom did God first reveal the name El Shaddai?
What does El Shaddai mean?

options: God of miraculous provision beyond human ability
practice.question: El Shaddai is the name of:

content.devotional: God first revealed Himself as El Shaddai to a 99-year-old man with a barren wife and told him he would be father of nations. The name meets us at the point of human impossibility. When your resources run out, when medicine has no answer, when hope seems gone — El Shaddai is just beginning to work.
content.grammar_note: El (אֵל) is the simple form of God — raw power. Shaddai (שַׁדַּי) is from a root meaning might or breast — one who nourishes with power. Together: the mighty God who nourishes and sustains beyond all human capacity.
content.pronunciation: El sha-DYE — stress on the second syllable of Shaddai

verse_connections.connection: I am El Shaddai — walk before me and be thou perfect — God's self-introduction to Abraham at the covenant of circumcision, when he was 99.
And God Almighty (El Shaddai) bless thee, and make thee fruitful — Isaac blesses Jacob with this name before he journeys to find a wife.
Job's story uses El Shaddai more than any other book — 31 times — because Job's suffering drives him to wrestle with God's all-sufficient power.

_vocabulary.meaning: Almighty, All-Sufficient — the nourishing, overpowering God
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: I am the Almighty God; walk before me, and be thou perfect.

_vocabulary.meaning: God — the raw power of divinity; the most ancient Hebrew name for God
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble.

_vocabulary.meaning: Covenant — the binding agreement between God and His people
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: And I will establish my covenant between me and thee.

vocabulary.title: Adonai — אֲדֹנָי
vocabulary.subtitle: Lord — Master and Sovereign
vocabulary.overview: Adonai means Lord or Master — it speaks of God's absolute sovereignty over all things. It is the word Jewish readers substitute aloud for the sacred YHWH when reading Scripture. While YHWH reveals God's personal, intimate name, Adonai declares His authority and lordship. When Isaiah saw the Lord seated on His throne in Isaiah 6, the word used is Adonai — the sovereign Master of the universe enthroned in glory.

content.meaning: Lord, Master, Sovereign — my Lord. Adonai is the plural of Adon (lord, master) with the suffix i (my). When used of God it is a plural of majesty: not my lords but My Supreme Lord. It emphasises absolute ownership and authority — God is Master of all.

practice.question: What does Adonai mean?
When Jewish readers encounter YHWH in the text, they say:
Isaiah saw the Lord (Adonai) seated on a throne in:

content.devotional: To call God Adonai is to declare yourself His servant. It is the word of surrender — You are Master, I am yours. Moses used it. David used it. Isaiah cried it in the throne room. Every prayer that begins with Lord is an echo of Adonai — an acknowledgment that He owns everything, including us.
content.grammar_note: Adonai ends in י (i — my) making it literally my lords or my Lord. The plural of adon (master) with the possessive — God is not just any lord but the Supreme Lord, MY Lord. A declaration of personal submission.
content.pronunciation: Ah-doh-NYE — stress on the final syllable
content.part_of_speech: Noun — plural of majesty with possessive suffix

verse_connections.connection: In the year that king Uzziah died I saw also the Lord (Adonai) sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up — the throne room vision uses Adonai, not YHWH.
O LORD (YHWH) our Lord (Adonai), how excellent is thy name — David uses both names together: the personal covenant name and the sovereign title.
And Abram said, Lord GOD (Adonai YHWH), what wilt thou give me — Abraham combines both names in his prayer, addressing God as both Master and covenant partner.

_vocabulary.meaning: Lord, Master — absolute sovereignty; substitute spoken for YHWH
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: I saw also the Lord sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up.

_vocabulary.meaning: Lord, master, owner — the root of Adonai
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Tremble, thou earth, at the presence of the Lord.

_vocabulary.meaning: Throne — the seat of divine sovereignty and royal judgment
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: God sitteth upon the throne of his holiness.

vocabulary.title: El Elyon — אֵל עֶלְיוֹן
vocabulary.subtitle: God Most High
vocabulary.overview: El Elyon — God Most High — is the name that declares God's supremacy above all other powers, rulers, and claims. It was first used by Melchizedek, the mysterious priest-king of Salem, when he blessed Abraham. It is the name that situates God above every earthly throne and heavenly power. In Daniel, the Most High rules in the kingdom of men and gives it to whomever He wills — El Elyon is sovereign over all of human history.

content.meaning: God Most High — the Highest One, supreme above all. Elyon comes from the root alah meaning to go up or ascend. El Elyon is the God who is above every other power — above kings, nations, angels, and any claim to authority. He rules from the highest place.

practice.question: Who first used the name El Elyon in Scripture?
What does Elyon mean?
What Hebrew root is Elyon connected to?

content.devotional: When everything around you claims authority over your life — governments, illness, financial pressure, fear — El Elyon reminds you who actually sits highest. Melchizedek blessed Abraham by this name after a great battle. In the aftermath of conflict, the first thing acknowledged was: God is Most High. Nothing won the battle except Him.
content.grammar_note: Elyon (עֶלְיוֹן) comes from the root עָלָה (alah — to ascend, to go up). The same root gives us the word aliyah — going up to Israel, or being called up to read Torah. God's name Most High is rooted in the concept of ascent and supremacy.
content.pronunciation: El el-YOHN — stress on the final syllable of Elyon

verse_connections.connection: And Melchizedek king of Salem... blessed him, and said, Blessed be Abram of the most high God (El Elyon) — the first use of this name in Scripture after Abraham's great victory.
I blessed the most High (Elyon) and I praised and honoured him that liveth for ever — Nebuchadnezzar's declaration after God humbled him.
He that dwelleth in the secret place of the most High (Elyon) shall abide under the shadow of the Almighty (Shaddai) — two great names joined in the psalm of divine protection.

_vocabulary.meaning: Most High — supreme above all powers, rulers, and authorities
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: He that dwelleth in the secret place of the most High shall abide under the shadow of the Almighty.

_vocabulary.meaning: Melchizedek — King of Righteousness; mysterious priest-king of Salem
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: And Melchizedek king of Salem brought forth bread and wine.

_vocabulary.meaning: To go up, to ascend, to rise — the root of Most High (Elyon)
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: And Moses went up unto God.

vocabulary.title: YHWH Yireh — יהוה יִרְאֶה
vocabulary.subtitle: The LORD Will Provide
vocabulary.overview: YHWH Yireh — The LORD Will Provide — is the name Abraham gave to the mountain where God stopped his hand from sacrificing Isaac and provided a ram instead. It is the name born out of the most severe test of faith in the Old Testament. Yireh comes from the Hebrew verb ra'ah — to see. The LORD sees the need before we voice it, sees the solution before we reach the crisis, and sees us even in our deepest valleys.

content.meaning: The LORD Will See / The LORD Will Provide. Yireh comes from ra'ah (to see). In Hebrew, to see a need and to provide for it are the same concept. When God sees your situation, provision is already on the way. This name was born on Mount Moriah — the same mountain where Solomon's Temple would later be built, and tradition holds, the same hill of Golgotha.

practice.question: What does Yireh mean?
Where did Abraham name God YHWH Yireh?
The Hebrew verb ra'ah (to see) also means:

content.devotional: Abraham named the mountain The LORD Will Provide — future tense. Not The LORD Provided (past) but Will Provide. He was declaring that what God did on that mountain would be done again, for others, in other crises, throughout all time. Every time you face an impossible need, you stand on Abraham's mountain — and the same God who saw then, sees now.
content.grammar_note: יִרְאֶה (Yireh) is the Qal imperfect third person masculine singular of רָאָה (ra'ah — to see). The imperfect tense in Hebrew expresses ongoing or future action: He will see, He is seeing, He sees continuously. The LORD's provision is not a one-time act but an ongoing, watchful care.
content.pronunciation: Yah-weh yir-EH — stress on the final syllable
content.part_of_speech: Noun phrase — name of God given by Abraham

verse_connections.connection: And Abraham called the name of that place Jehovah-jireh (YHWH Yireh): as it is said to this day, In the mount of the LORD it shall be seen — the birth of this name.
And Abraham said, My son, God will provide himself a lamb for a burnt offering — Abraham's faith declaration before he saw the provision.
But my God shall supply all your need according to his riches in glory by Christ Jesus — the New Testament echo of YHWH Yireh, now fulfilled in Christ.

_vocabulary.meaning: He will see / He will provide — to see a need is to meet it
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: God will provide himself a lamb for a burnt offering.

_vocabulary.meaning: To see, to look, to perceive, to provide — seeing and providing are one act
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Thou God seest me.

_vocabulary.meaning: Lamb — the sacrificial animal; points forward to the Lamb of God
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Behold the fire and the wood: but where is the lamb for a burnt offering?

vocabulary.title: Bara — בָּרָא
vocabulary.subtitle: Creation Out of Nothing
vocabulary.overview: Bara is one of the most theologically significant verbs in the entire Bible. It means to create out of nothing — and remarkably, in all of Scripture it is used exclusively with God as its subject. Humans make, build, form, and craft — but only God bara. When Genesis 1:1 declares that God created the heavens and the earth, the word is bara — an act no human hand could ever replicate.

content.meaning: To create out of nothing — ex nihilo creation. Used exclusively of God in Scripture. Never used of human creativity or craftsmanship. When God bara, something brand new exists that never existed before in any form.

practice.question: Who is ALWAYS the subject of the verb bara?

options: It means to build with hands
It means creation out of nothing — only God does it
It is only used in the Psalms
practice.question: What makes bara different from other Hebrew words for making?
How many times does bara appear in the Old Testament?

content.devotional: There is a category of action that belongs to God alone — bara. You can make things, build things, rearrange things. But you cannot create from nothing. Only God can speak into absolute void and produce something real. This is why the resurrection is possible, why new birth is real, and why God can make all things new. He is the only One who bara.
content.grammar_note: Bara appears in the Qal stem — the simple active form. The subject is always God (Elohim, YHWH, or a divine pronoun). This grammatical pattern is consistent across all 54 occurrences — no human ever bara. The word distinguishes divine creation from human making (asah — עָשָׂה).
content.pronunciation: bah-RAH — stress on the final syllable

verse_connections.connection: In the beginning God bara — the very first verb describing divine action is bara. Before anything existed, God created.
But now thus saith the LORD that bara thee, O Jacob — God's act of creating Israel uses bara, the same word as Genesis 1.
Create (bara) in me a clean heart, O God — David uses bara for the only human request that uses this word: only God can create a new heart.

_vocabulary.meaning: To create out of nothing — used only of God, never of human making
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth.

_vocabulary.meaning: To make, to do, to accomplish — human and divine making from existing materials
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: And God made the firmament.

_vocabulary.meaning: To form, to fashion — as a potter shapes clay; used of God forming man
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: And the LORD God formed man of the dust of the ground.

vocabulary.title: Shamayim — שָׁמַיִם
vocabulary.subtitle: The Heavens Above
vocabulary.overview: Shamayim is the Hebrew word for heaven or sky — and like many profound Hebrew words, it is always plural. The heavens cannot be singular in Hebrew — they are always shamayim, never shameh. Some rabbis see in this plural a hint of multiple heavens: the atmospheric sky, outer space, and the dwelling place of God. Paul speaks of being caught up to the third heaven (2 Corinthians 12:2) — an idea rooted in this Hebrew plurality.

content.meaning: Heaven, heavens, sky — always plural in Hebrew. The dwelling place of God, the atmospheric sky, and outer space are all captured in this one word. The plural signals infinite expanse and divine fullness.

options: The heavens are too vast and layered to be singular
There are many gods in heaven
It refers only to the sky
practice.question: Shamayim is always plural. What does this suggest?

options: Atmospheric sky, outer space, and God's dwelling
practice.question: What are the three layers some rabbis see in Shamayim?
Which apostle spoke of being caught up to the third heaven?

content.devotional: Every time you look up — at clouds, stars, or blue sky — you are looking at shamayim. The same word covers it all. When Scripture says God dwells in the heavens, it does not mean He is far away. Shamayim is above you right now. The plural heavens surround the singular earth — God's presence encompasses our existence from every direction above.
content.grammar_note: Shamayim is a plural tantum — a word that only exists in plural form, like the English word 'scissors.' There is no singular shameh in biblical Hebrew. This grammatical fact carries theological weight: the heavens are too vast, too layered, too full of God's glory to be captured in a singular form.
content.pronunciation: sha-MY-im — stress on the second syllable

verse_connections.connection: In the beginning God created the shamayim and the earth — heaven is the first thing named after God Himself in Scripture.
The shamayim declare the glory of God — the plural heavens are a plural testimony to divine glory.
Thus saith the LORD, The shamayim is my throne — God's throne room is the heavens themselves.

_vocabulary.meaning: Heaven, sky — always plural; the expanse above and God's dwelling place
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth.

_vocabulary.meaning: Earth, land, ground — the created realm beneath the heavens
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth.

_vocabulary.meaning: Firmament, expanse — the stretched-out dome of sky God made on day two
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: And God said, Let there be a firmament in the midst of the waters.

vocabulary.title: Ruach — רוּחַ
vocabulary.subtitle: Spirit, Wind and Breath
vocabulary.overview: Ruach is one of the richest and most layered words in all of Hebrew Scripture. It means spirit, wind, and breath simultaneously — and the context determines which meaning applies, though often all three overlap intentionally. The Spirit of God hovered over the waters in Genesis 1. God breathed ruach into Adam's nostrils. The valley of dry bones came alive when ruach entered them. One word carries the full weight of divine animation.

content.meaning: Spirit, wind, breath — three meanings in one word. The divine ruach animates, moves, empowers, and creates. It is the breath of God that gives life, the wind of God that parts seas, and the Spirit of God that fills prophets and kings.

practice.question: Ruach can mean all of the following EXCEPT:
What grammatical gender is ruach in Hebrew?
In Genesis 1:2, what was the Ruach Elohim doing over the waters?

content.devotional: When you breathe right now, you are participating in the oldest act in Scripture — receiving ruach. God breathed into Adam and he became a living soul. Every breath you take is a gift of the same ruach that hovered over the void. You are alive because of ruach. And when Jesus breathed on His disciples and said 'Receive the Holy Spirit' — He was repeating Genesis 2:7 in the upper room.
content.grammar_note: Ruach is feminine in Hebrew — suggesting the nurturing, life-giving aspect of the Spirit. The phrase Ruach Elohim (Spirit of God) appears in Genesis 1:2 with the verb merachefet (hovering) — a feminine participle, confirming ruach is feminine. Some theologians connect this to the Spirit as the life-giver and nurturer.
content.pronunciation: ROO-akh — the final ch is a guttural, like Bach

verse_connections.connection: And the Ruach Elohim (Spirit of God) hovered over the face of the waters — ruach present before the first word of creation was spoken.
And the LORD God breathed (nishmat ruach) into his nostrils the breath of life — ruach is the direct cause of human life.
Come from the four winds (ruach), O ruach, and breathe upon these slain — Ezekiel uses ruach three ways in one verse: wind, breath, and Spirit.

_vocabulary.meaning: Spirit, wind, breath — the animating breath and Spirit of God
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: And the Spirit of God moved upon the face of the waters.

_vocabulary.meaning: Breath, soul — the specific breath God breathed into Adam's nostrils
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: And breathed into his nostrils the breath of life.

_vocabulary.meaning: To live, to be alive — the result of receiving ruach and neshamah
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: I will cause breath to enter into you, and ye shall live.

vocabulary.title: Hesed — חֶסֶד
vocabulary.subtitle: The Covenant Love of God
vocabulary.overview: Hesed is arguably the single most important covenant word in the entire Old Testament — and it has no perfect English equivalent. It is translated variously as lovingkindness, mercy, steadfast love, and loyal love. But hesed is deeper than all of these. It is the love that does not quit, the kindness that keeps its promise, the mercy that returns even when it has been rejected. Psalm 136 repeats it 26 times in a row: His hesed endures forever.

content.meaning: Lovingkindness, steadfast love, covenant mercy — the loyal love that keeps its promises. Hesed is not merely an emotion but a commitment. It is love expressed through action, especially when the recipient is undeserving. It is the word that defines God's covenant relationship with Israel and with us.

practice.question: Which English word best captures hesed?
How many times does 'His hesed endures forever' appear in Psalm 136?
Hesed is often paired with which other Hebrew word?

content.devotional: There is a love that does not depend on your performance — hesed. God's hesed toward you was not activated by your goodness and cannot be deactivated by your failures. It is covenant love — He bound Himself to you and His word does not return void. The 26-fold repetition of 'His hesed endures forever' in Psalm 136 is not poetic excess. It is the most important truth in the universe stated 26 times so you will not forget it.
content.grammar_note: Hesed is often paired with emet (אֱמֶת — truth/faithfulness): hesed ve-emet — lovingkindness and truth. Together they form a hendiadys (two words expressing one concept): faithful lovingkindness. This pairing appears throughout the Psalms and captures the dual nature of God's covenant love: it is both warm and reliable.
content.pronunciation: KHEH-sed — the initial ch is guttural, like Bach

verse_connections.connection: O give thanks unto the LORD; for he is good: for his hesed endureth for ever — the refrain repeated 26 times in this psalm alone.
It is of the LORD's hesed that we are not consumed, because his compassions fail not. They are new every morning — hesed is the reason we survive our darkest nights.
What doth the LORD require of thee, but to do justly, and to love hesed, and to walk humbly — hesed is not just what God shows us but what He calls us to show others.

_vocabulary.meaning: Lovingkindness, steadfast love — loyal covenant love that never quits
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: O give thanks unto the LORD; for he is good: for his mercy endureth for ever.

_vocabulary.meaning: Truth, faithfulness, reliability — often paired with hesed as faithful love
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: All the paths of the LORD are mercy and truth unto such as keep his covenant.

_vocabulary.meaning: Compassion, tender mercies — from the root for womb; motherly deep love
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: It is of the LORD's mercies that we are not consumed.

vocabulary.title: Shalom — שָׁלוֹם
vocabulary.subtitle: Wholeness, Completeness and Peace
vocabulary.overview: Shalom is the most recognised Hebrew word in the world — but its depth is almost never understood. Shalom does not merely mean the absence of conflict. It means wholeness, completeness, nothing missing and nothing broken. When God blesses with shalom, He is saying: every part of your life is whole, every relationship is restored, every lack is supplied. The Aaronic blessing ends with shalom — and it is the final word God speaks over His people.

content.meaning: Peace, wholeness, completeness, welfare — nothing missing and nothing broken. Shalom is the state of total wellbeing: spiritually, physically, relationally, and materially. It is not passive but active — a dynamic state of flourishing in every dimension of life.

options: Wholeness — nothing missing and nothing broken
practice.question: What does shalom really mean beyond just peace?
What Hebrew root is shalom connected to?
The name Jerusalem contains the root of shalom. What does it mean?

content.devotional: When you say shalom to someone, you are not just saying hello or goodbye. You are pronouncing a blessing: may you be whole, may nothing be missing, may nothing be broken in your life. Jesus said 'Peace (shalom) I leave with you' — not the world's version of temporary calm, but the covenant shalom that makes everything complete. The Prince of Shalom (Isaiah 9:6) came to restore what was broken in the garden.
content.grammar_note: Shalom comes from the root shalem (שָׁלֵם — to be complete, to be whole). The same root gives us shalam (to repay, to make whole) and the name Jerusalem (Yeru-shalem — City of Peace/Wholeness). Shalom is not just a greeting — it is a declaration of intended wholeness over the person you greet.
content.pronunciation: sha-LOME — stress on the final syllable

verse_connections.connection: The LORD bless thee and keep thee... and give thee shalom — the Aaronic blessing ends with shalom as God's final word over His people.
His name shall be called... Prince of Shalom (Sar Shalom) — the Messiah's title declares His mission: to restore total wholeness to a broken world.
Peace (shalom) I leave with you, my peace I give unto you — Jesus' farewell gift is the covenant shalom the world cannot give or take away.

_vocabulary.meaning: Peace, wholeness, completeness — nothing missing and nothing broken
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: The LORD lift up his countenance upon thee, and give thee peace.

_vocabulary.meaning: Complete, whole, at peace — the root of shalom
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: And Jacob came to Shalem, a city of Shechem.

_vocabulary.meaning: Blessing — the spoken declaration of God's favour and intended wholeness
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: On this wise ye shall bless the children of Israel.

vocabulary.title: Berit — בְּרִית
vocabulary.subtitle: The Covenant Bond
vocabulary.overview: Berit — covenant — is the structural framework of the entire Bible. Every major movement of Scripture is organised around a covenant: Noah, Abraham, Moses, David, and the New Covenant. Berit is not a contract between equals — it is a binding commitment, often sealed with blood, that creates a relationship where none existed before or restores one that was broken. The Bible is literally named after this word: the Old and New Testaments are the Old and New Covenants — Old and New Berit.

content.meaning: Covenant, binding agreement, solemn commitment — a relationship sealed with an oath and often with blood. Berit creates a new reality: two parties who were separate become bound together. God's covenants are always initiated by Him and are expressions of grace, not negotiated contracts.

options: The ancient practice of cutting animals and walking between them
practice.question: What does the phrase karat berit (cut a covenant) refer to?
In Genesis 15, who walked between the cut animals?
The Old and New Testaments are literally the Old and New what?

content.devotional: In Genesis 15, God put Abraham into a deep sleep and walked through the cut animals alone. In ancient covenant ceremony, both parties walked through — binding each other. But God walked through alone. He bound Himself without binding Abraham. He said: this covenant depends entirely on Me. It cannot be broken by your failure. This is berit — the one-sided grace covenant that holds you not because of your faithfulness but because of His.
content.grammar_note: The Hebrew phrase for making a covenant is karat berit (כָּרַת בְּרִית) — literally to cut a covenant. This comes from the ancient practice of cutting animals in half and walking between them (as in Genesis 15) — symbolising: may what happened to these animals happen to me if I break this covenant. God Himself walked between the pieces for Abraham, taking the oath upon Himself alone.
content.pronunciation: beh-REET — stress on the final syllable

verse_connections.connection: In the same day the LORD made (cut) a berit with Abram — the foundational covenant of the Bible, sealed by God walking through the pieces alone.
Behold, the days come, saith the LORD, that I will make a new berit with the house of Israel — the first announcement of the New Covenant in Scripture.
This cup is the new berit in my blood, which is shed for you — Jesus identifies Himself as the blood that seals the new covenant Jeremiah foretold.

_vocabulary.meaning: Covenant — the binding commitment sealed with oath and blood
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: In the same day the LORD made a covenant with Abram.

_vocabulary.meaning: To cut — as in karat berit (cut a covenant); to cut off, to make
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: In the same day the LORD made (cut) a covenant with Abram.

_vocabulary.meaning: Blood — the seal of covenant; life is in the blood (Leviticus 17:11)
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: And Moses took the blood, and sprinkled it on the people, and said, Behold the blood of the covenant.

vocabulary.title: Av
vocabulary.subtitle: Father
vocabulary.overview: The Hebrew word for father — one of the most intimate words in all of Scripture.

practice.question: What does Av (אָב) mean?
How many times does Av appear in the Old Testament?

practice.answer: Avot (אָבוֹת) — meaning fathers or ancestors
practice.question: What is the plural form of Av?

content.devotional: Av — father. The very first word in the Strong's Concordance is Av. It is the foundation word of relationship in the Bible. God revealed Himself progressively: first as Elohim the Creator, then as YHWH the Covenant God, and finally — through Jesus — as Av, Father. When Jesus taught His disciples to pray, He began with two words that would have been revolutionary: Our Father. Abba. The God of the universe is your Av.
content.grammar_note: One of the shortest and most ancient words in Hebrew. The construct form is Avi (אָבִי) — my father. The plural is Avot (אָבוֹת) — fathers, ancestors.
content.pronunciation: AV (rhymes with 'calf')

verse_connections.connection: Therefore shall a man leave his Av (father) and his mother — the first use of Av in a family context, establishing the family order God designed.
Like as a father (Av) pitieth his children, so the LORD pitieth them that fear him — God's fatherly compassion described using Av.
The Messiah is called Everlasting Father (Av Ad) — the Son who carries the nature of the eternal Av.

_vocabulary.meaning: Father
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Like as a father pitieth his children, so the LORD pitieth them that fear him.

vocabulary.title: Em
vocabulary.subtitle: Mother
vocabulary.overview: The Hebrew word for mother — a word of profound tenderness woven through Scripture.

practice.question: What does Em (אֵם) mean?
Which word shares a root with Em, meaning faithfulness?

practice.answer: Sarah, Rebekah, Leah, and Rachel — the four matriarchs
practice.question: What are the four Imahot (mothers) of Israel?

content.devotional: Em — mother. In Hebrew thought, the mother is the heartbeat of the home. The word Em shares its root with the word Emunah (אֱמוּנָה) — faithfulness, trust. A mother is one who is faithful, who can be trusted absolutely. Isaiah uses the image of an Em to describe God's own tenderness: 'Can a woman forget her nursing child... yet I will not forget you.' When God wanted to describe His most unbreakable love, He reached for the word Em.
content.grammar_note: Em is the feminine counterpart to Av. The construct form is Imi (אִמִּי) — my mother. The plural is Imahot (אִמָּהוֹת) — the matriarchs. Sarah, Rebekah, Leah and Rachel are called the four Imahot of Israel.
content.pronunciation: AIM (like the English word 'aim')

verse_connections.connection: Honour thy father (Av) and thy mother (Em) — Em appears alongside Av in the fifth commandment, establishing the equal honour due to both parents.
Can a woman forget her sucking child, that she should not have compassion on the son of her womb? — God uses the Em image to describe His unforgettable love.
The words of king Lemuel, the prophecy that his mother (Em) taught him — the famous Proverbs 31 woman begins with a mother's teaching.

_vocabulary.meaning: Mother
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Can a woman forget her sucking child, that she should not have compassion on the son of her womb?

vocabulary.title: Ben
vocabulary.subtitle: Son
vocabulary.overview: The Hebrew word for son — a word that carries covenant, inheritance, and identity.

practice.question: What does Ben (בֵּן) mean?
What does Ben-Elohim mean?

practice.answer: Binyamin (בִּנְיָמִין) — son of the right hand
practice.question: What does the name Benjamin mean in Hebrew?

content.devotional: Ben — son. In Hebrew culture, a son carried the name, the inheritance, and the mission of the father. To be called someone's ben was to share their identity completely. The most profound use of Ben in all of Scripture is Ben-Elohim — son of God. When God declared over Jesus at His baptism 'This is my beloved Son,' the Hebrew ear would have heard: this one shares my nature, my name, my everything. Every believer is called a ben through adoption — heirs together with the firstborn Son.
content.grammar_note: Ben is one of the most frequent nouns in the Hebrew Bible. The construct form Ben is used in names: Benjamin (בִּנְיָמִין = son of the right hand), Bethlehem (בֵּית לֶחֶם = house of bread). The plural is Banim (בָּנִים) — sons, children. Ben-Elohim means son of God.
content.pronunciation: BEN (rhymes with 'pen')

verse_connections.connection: For whom the LORD loveth he correcteth; even as a father the son (Ben) in whom he delighteth — the father-son relationship as the image of God's discipline and love.
Thou art my Son (Ben); this day have I begotten thee — the royal Psalm prophesying the Messiah as God's Ben, quoted in the New Testament of Jesus.
Take now thy son (Ben), thine only son Isaac, whom thou lovest — the first use of Ben in a sacrificial context, foreshadowing the Father and Son at Calvary.

_vocabulary.meaning: Son
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Thou art my Son; this day have I begotten thee.

vocabulary.title: Bat
vocabulary.subtitle: Daughter
vocabulary.overview: The Hebrew word for daughter — a word of beauty, inheritance, and belonging.

practice.question: What does Bat (בַּת) mean?
What does Bat-Zion mean?

practice.answer: Bat-Sheva (בַּת שֶׁבַע) — daughter of an oath
practice.question: What does the name Bathsheba mean in Hebrew?

content.devotional: Bat — daughter. The prophets used Bat-Zion — Daughter of Zion — as the most tender address for God's people. It is the voice of a father calling to his beloved daughter. Zephaniah writes: 'Sing, O Daughter of Zion... the LORD thy God in the midst of thee is mighty; he will save, he will rejoice over thee with joy.' The God of the universe sings over His Bat with joy. You are not a servant, not a subject — you are a daughter, and your Father sings.
content.grammar_note: Bat is the feminine form of Ben. It appears in the famous phrase Bat-Zion (בַּת צִיּוֹן) — Daughter of Zion — used by the prophets to address Jerusalem and God's people tenderly. The plural is Banot (בָּנוֹת) — daughters. Bat also appears in names: Bathsheba (בַּת שֶׁבַע = daughter of an oath).
content.pronunciation: BAT (rhymes with 'cat')

verse_connections.connection: Sing, O daughter (Bat) of Zion; shout, O Israel — God addresses His people as Bat-Zion, His beloved daughter, calling her to rejoice.
The king's daughter (Bat) is all glorious within — a Messianic psalm describing the beauty of God's people as a daughter brought to the King.
The daughters (Banot) of Zelophehad speak right — the first time in Scripture daughters claimed their inheritance, establishing that daughters are full heirs.

_vocabulary.meaning: Daughter
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Sing, O daughter of Zion; shout, O Israel; be glad and rejoice with all the heart.

vocabulary.title: Hallelujah
vocabulary.subtitle: Praise the LORD
vocabulary.overview: The most universal word of worship — spoken in every language on earth.

practice.question: What does Hallelujah literally mean?
Yah (יָהּ) in Hallelujah is a shortened form of which name?

practice.answer: Four times (Revelation 19:1, 3, 4, 6) — the final great chorus of heaven
practice.question: How many times does Hallelujah appear in the book of Revelation?

content.devotional: Hallelujah is the only Hebrew word sung in every language on earth without translation. No one says 'praise the LORD' in their hymns — they say Hallelujah. God preserved this word across every culture and tongue. It appears 24 times in Scripture, concentrated in the Psalms. The book of Revelation records the heavenly choir crying Hallelujah four times — the final great chorus of all creation returning praise to its Maker.
content.grammar_note: A compound of Hallelu (הַלְלוּ — plural imperative of halal, to praise) + Yah (יָהּ — the shortened form of YHWH). Literally: 'You all, praise Yah!' It is a command addressed to a group.
content.pronunciation: Hal-le-lu-YAH (stress on the final syllable)

verse_connections.connection: Let every thing that hath breath praise the LORD (Hallelujah) — the final verse of the entire Psalter ends with Hallelujah, the ultimate conclusion of all worship.
And I heard as it were the voice of a great multitude... saying, Alleluia: for the Lord God omnipotent reigneth — the heavenly Hallelujah at the culmination of history.
Praise ye the LORD (Hallelujah). Praise, O ye servants of the LORD — the opening of the Hallel psalms (113-118) sung at every Passover.

_vocabulary.meaning: Praise the LORD
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Let every thing that hath breath praise the LORD. Praise ye the LORD.

vocabulary.title: Amen
vocabulary.subtitle: So Be It — Truly
vocabulary.overview: The word that seals every prayer — rooted in the Hebrew word for faithfulness.

practice.question: What is the root meaning of Amen?
Which word shares the same root as Amen?

practice.answer: It is firm, true, and reliable — I stand on this and trust it completely
practice.question: What does saying Amen to a prayer or promise declare?

content.devotional: Amen is not a full stop at the end of prayer — it is a declaration of faith. It means: I believe this is true. I stand on this. So let it be. The root aman is the same root as the word for a nursing mother (Omenet — אֹמֶנֶת) — someone completely reliable, utterly trustworthy. When you say Amen to God's promises, you are saying: You are as faithful as a mother to her nursing child. I trust You completely.
content.grammar_note: From the root אָמַן (aman — to be firm, faithful, reliable). Related to Emunah (אֱמוּנָה — faith, faithfulness) and Emet (אֱמֶת — truth). When you say Amen you are saying: this is firm, this is true, so let it be.
content.pronunciation: Ah-MEN (Hebrew) or AY-men (English tradition)

verse_connections.connection: The first double Amen in Scripture — the woman responds Amen, Amen to the priestly oath, signifying complete acceptance and trust.
All the people shall say Amen — twelve times the nation collectively confirmed the covenant curses with Amen, sealing their agreement with God.
These things saith the Amen, the faithful and true witness — Jesus calls Himself 'the Amen,' the ultimate embodiment of divine faithfulness.

_vocabulary.meaning: So be it, truly, faithful
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: And all the people shall say, Amen.

vocabulary.title: Torah
vocabulary.subtitle: Instruction — The Law
vocabulary.overview: Often translated as Law, Torah truly means instruction — the loving teaching of a Father.

practice.question: What does Torah literally mean?
What is the root verb of Torah?

practice.answer: Psalm 119 — the longest chapter in the Bible, with 176 verses all meditating on God's instruction
practice.question: Which Psalm is entirely devoted to the beauty of Torah?

content.devotional: Torah does not mean law in the sense of cold legal code. It means the instruction of a loving Father who teaches His children how to live well. The root yarah means to shoot an arrow — Torah is the instruction that hits the mark. Psalm 119, the longest chapter in the Bible, is entirely devoted to the beauty of Torah. The psalmist does not endure Torah — he delights in it: 'Oh how I love thy Torah! It is my meditation all the day.'
content.grammar_note: From the root יָרָה (yarah — to throw, to shoot, to direct, to teach). Torah means directed instruction — the teaching that hits the mark. Often translated 'law' in English, but law is too cold. A better translation is 'instruction' or 'teaching.'
content.pronunciation: Toh-RAH (stress on second syllable)

verse_connections.connection: O how love I thy law (Torah)! it is my meditation all the day — the psalmist's response to Torah is not reluctant obedience but passionate love.
My son, forget not my law (Torah); but let thine heart keep my commandments — Torah framed as a father's instruction to his beloved son.
For out of Zion shall go forth the law (Torah), and the word of the LORD from Jerusalem — the Messianic age described as a new outpouring of Torah to all nations.

_vocabulary.meaning: Instruction, teaching, the Law
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: O how love I thy law! it is my meditation all the day.

vocabulary.title: Mitzvah
vocabulary.subtitle: Commandment — Connection
vocabulary.overview: More than a rule — a Mitzvah is a divine connection point between God and His people.

practice.question: What does Mitzvah mean?
How many Mitzvot does Jewish tradition count in the Torah?

practice.answer: Tzavta — meaning connection, companionship, being together with God
practice.question: What Aramaic word is Mitzvah related to, and what does it mean?

content.devotional: The rabbis taught that the word Mitzvah is related to the Aramaic word tzavta — meaning connection, companionship, being together. A Mitzvah is not just a command to obey — it is a point of connection with God. When you do a Mitzvah, you are not just following a rule: you are meeting God. The 613 commandments are 613 opportunities to connect with the One who commanded them. Jesus summarized all the Mitzvot in two: love God with all your heart, and love your neighbour as yourself.
content.grammar_note: From the root צָוָה (tzavah — to command, to charge, to commission). The plural is Mitzvot (מִצְוֹת). Jewish tradition counts 613 Mitzvot in the Torah. The Shema calls Israel to love YHWH and keep His Mitzvot.
content.pronunciation: Mitz-VAH (stress on second syllable)

verse_connections.connection: And it shall be our righteousness, if we observe to do all these commandments (Mitzvot) before the LORD our God — keeping the Mitzvot is described as righteousness.
Then shall I not be ashamed, when I have respect unto all thy commandments (Mitzvot) — the Mitzvot as the path of confidence before God.
On these two commandments hang all the law and the prophets — Jesus distilling all 613 Mitzvot into love for God and love for neighbour.

_vocabulary.meaning: Commandment, divine instruction
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: And it shall be our righteousness, if we observe to do all these commandments before the LORD our God.

vocabulary.title: Mashiach
vocabulary.subtitle: The Anointed One — Messiah
vocabulary.overview: The promised Anointed One — the word that threads through all of Scripture toward its fulfilment.

practice.question: What does Mashiach mean?
What is the Greek equivalent of Mashiach?

practice.answer: Prophet, Priest, and King — the Mashiach holds all three forever
practice.question: Which three offices were filled by anointing in Israel, all fulfilled in the Mashiach?

content.devotional: In ancient Israel, three kinds of people were anointed: prophets, priests, and kings. The Mashiach was the One who would hold all three offices at once — prophet, priest, and king forever. Every anointed king of Israel was a partial fulfilment and a forward pointer. When Simeon held the infant Jesus in the Temple and said 'mine eyes have seen thy salvation,' he was holding the Mashiach — the one anointed not with oil but with the Spirit without measure. The hope of every page of the Old Testament is Mashiach.
content.grammar_note: From the root מָשַׁח (mashach — to anoint with oil). Mashiach means the anointed one — one set apart and empowered by the pouring of oil. Kings, priests, and prophets were all anointed. The Greek translation is Christos — Christ. Jesus = Yeshua HaMashiach = Jesus the Christ.
content.pronunciation: Mah-SHEE-akh (the final kh is a soft guttural, like Bach)

verse_connections.connection: Know therefore and understand, that from the going forth of the commandment to restore and to build Jerusalem unto the Messiah (Mashiach) the Prince — Daniel's precise prophecy of the Mashiach's arrival.
The kings of the earth set themselves... against the LORD, and against his anointed (Mashiach) — the Messianic Psalm prophesying the rejection and ultimate triumph of the Mashiach.
The Spirit of the Lord GOD is upon me; because the LORD hath anointed (mashach) me — the Mashiach's own declaration, quoted by Jesus in Luke 4 as fulfilled in their hearing.

_vocabulary.meaning: The Anointed One, Messiah
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Know therefore and understand, that from the going forth of the commandment to restore and to build Jerusalem unto the Messiah the Prince.

vocabulary.title: Navi
vocabulary.subtitle: Prophet — Spokesman
vocabulary.overview: A prophet in Hebrew is not primarily a predictor of the future — he is a spokesman for God.

options: A spokesman who speaks for God
practice.question: What is the primary meaning of Navi?
What is the second section of the Hebrew Bible called?

practice.answer: Exodus 7:1 — God tells Moses that Aaron will be his navi (spokesman) to Pharaoh
practice.question: Which verse proves a Navi is a spokesman, not just a predictor?

content.devotional: A Navi is not primarily a fortune teller — he is a forth-teller, a spokesman. When God called Moses and Moses said he could not speak well, God replied: 'Aaron shall be thy spokesman (navi) to the people.' That is the definition of a Navi: one who speaks on behalf of another. Every Navi in Scripture was first a listener before they were a speaker. They heard from God in the secret place before they spoke to the people in the public place. The greatest Navi Moses declared: 'The LORD thy God will raise up unto thee a Prophet like unto me.' That Prophet is Yeshua HaMashiach.
content.grammar_note: Possibly from the root נָבַע (nava — to bubble up, to pour forth) or from the Akkadian nabu (to call, to announce). The Navi is one through whom God's words bubble up and pour forth. The plural is Neviim (נְבִיאִים) — the second section of the Hebrew Bible is called the Neviim.
content.pronunciation: Nah-VEE (stress on second syllable)

verse_connections.connection: I will raise them up a Prophet (Navi) from among their brethren, like unto thee — Moses' prophecy of the ultimate Navi, fulfilled in Jesus according to Acts 3:22.
Surely the Lord GOD will do nothing, but he revealeth his secret unto his servants the prophets (Neviim) — God's pattern of speaking through His Neviim before He acts.
And the LORD said unto Moses... Aaron thy brother shall be thy prophet (Navi) — the clearest definition of Navi as spokesman, one who speaks on behalf of another.

_vocabulary.meaning: Prophet, spokesman for God
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: I will raise them up a Prophet from among their brethren, like unto thee, and will put my words in his mouth.

vocabulary.title: Davar
vocabulary.subtitle: Word — Thing — Matter
vocabulary.overview: In Hebrew, a word and a thing are the same — because God's Word always becomes reality.

options: It is the longest word in Hebrew
It means both word and thing/reality
It only refers to God's speech
practice.question: What makes Davar unique among Hebrew words?
What is the Hebrew name for the book of Deuteronomy?

practice.answer: Because when God speaks, His word immediately becomes reality — there is no gap between His speaking and the thing existing
practice.question: Why does Davar mean both word and thing in Hebrew?

content.devotional: In Greek thought, words and things are separate: you say a word, and then separately, things happen. In Hebrew thought, they are the same. Davar is both word and thing — because when God speaks a davar, it immediately IS. 'He spake, and it was done' (Psalm 33:9). The Gospel of John opens: 'In the beginning was the Word (Logos in Greek, but Davar in Hebrew thought) — and the Word was God.' The eternal Davar of God took on flesh. Every promise God has spoken is a davar — and in Hebrew, a davar is already a thing. What God has said is as real as what already exists.
content.grammar_note: From the root דָּבַר (davar — to speak, to declare). Remarkably, davar means both word AND thing/event. This is unique to Hebrew — the same word covers both the spoken word and the reality it creates. The plural is Devarim (דְּבָרִים) — the Hebrew name for the book of Deuteronomy.
content.pronunciation: Dah-VAR (stress on second syllable)

verse_connections.connection: By the word (Davar) of the LORD were the heavens made — creation itself is a Davar, a spoken word that became a solid thing.
So shall my word (Davar) be that goeth forth out of my mouth: it shall not return unto me void — God's Davar is a self-fulfilling reality, guaranteed to accomplish its mission.
Then the word (Davar) of the LORD came unto me — the classic formula of prophetic revelation. The Davar of YHWH arriving as an event, not just information.

_vocabulary.meaning: Word, thing, matter, event
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: So shall my word be that goeth forth out of my mouth: it shall not return unto me void.
